The vignette's numbers correctly isolate the abnormal third measurement (only 1 finger hyoid-to-thyroid) while keeping the other two normal. Medicine is accurate and unambiguous; correct answer and all per-choice rationales are sound. Safe to publish.

questions.stem: A 64-year-old woman with a large fixed anterior neck mass from recurrent thyroid carcinoma, prior neck radiation, and stridor at rest presents for tumor debulking. Imaging shows tracheal deviation and narrowing. She is cooperative and oxygenates well on room air at rest. You plan an awake flexible fiberoptic (bronchoscopic) intubation. Which principle is MOST important to the safety of this technique?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Maintain spontaneous ventilation and avoid over-sedation so the patient continues to protect and ventilate her own airway throughout the procedure
choices.rationale: Correct. The central safety advantage of an awake technique is that spontaneous ventilation and airway-protective reflexes are preserved. Over-sedation risks loss of the airway in a patient who may be impossible to mask-ventilate or rescue, defeating the purpose of staying awake.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Administer a full induction dose of propofol and rocuronium before passing the scope to optimize intubating conditions
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Induction with a hypnotic plus a neuromuscular blocker abolishes spontaneous ventilation and converts this into an asleep intubation, the precise scenario the awake plan is designed to avoid in an anticipated difficult airway with airway compromise.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Skip airway topicalization to save time, relying on deep sedation alone to blunt airway reflexes
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Adequate topical anesthesia of the upper airway (and/or nerve blocks of the glossopharyngeal and superior laryngeal nerves) is what permits patient comfort and cooperation WITHOUT deep sedation. Substituting deep sedation for topicalization endangers spontaneous ventilation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Position the patient supine and apply continuous cricoid pressure throughout scope passage to improve the view
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Cricoid pressure can distort the glottic anatomy and impede passage of the fiberoptic scope, and it is not a core safety principle of awake fiberoptic intubation. The defining safety feature is preserved spontaneous ventilation, not cricoid pressure.

questions.explanation: Awake flexible fiberoptic intubation is the classic technique for the anticipated difficult airway, especially with distorted anatomy from tumor, prior surgery, or radiation, because the flexible scope can navigate a non-aligned, narrowed airway while the patient continues to breathe. Its entire margin of safety rests on keeping the patient spontaneously ventilating with intact protective reflexes; if intubation fails, the patient can still oxygenate and the airway is not lost. This requires meticulous topicalization (atomized/nebulized lidocaine, scope side-port 'spray-as-you-go,' and/or glossopharyngeal, superior laryngeal, and recurrent laryngeal nerve blocks) so the patient tolerates the procedure with minimal sedation. Over-sedation is the principal hazard, because losing spontaneous ventilation in a patient who may be unmaskable converts a controlled awake plan into a can't-intubate/can't-oxygenate emergency.

source.title: Awake Fiberoptic Intubation - OpenAnesthesia

verdict.reason: Source loads and is public (OpenAnesthesia keywords page on awake fiberoptic intubation, last updated 10/28/2025). It directly supports correct answer A: the page states patients "must be able to spontaneously maintain their airway at all times during intubation" and that induction agents are administered only AFTER ETT placement is confirmed. Medicine is correct and unambiguous: maintaining spontaneous ventilation and avoiding over-sedation is THE foundational safety principle of awake FOI, critical in this fixed-airway/possible CICV patient. Distractors are all clearly wrong and well-explained: B (full induction + rocuronium before the scope) defeats the purpose of an awake technique and risks total obstruction in a patient who cannot be ventilated; C (skipping topicalization, relying on deep sedation) is the exact over-sedation hazard to avoid and topicalization is essential; D (supine + continuous cricoid pressure) is not the key principle—upright positioning is preferred for a compromised airway and cricoid pressure can degrade the fiberoptic view. Rationales accurate. Safe to publish.

questions.stem: A 47-year-old man has undergone an 8-hour anterior cervical spine fusion in the prone position. He had a documented difficult intubation (Cormack-Lehane grade 3), and now has facial and airway edema. You judge reintubation, if needed, would be difficult. Following an at-risk-airway extubation strategy, you plan to extubate over an airway exchange catheter (AEC). Which combination of measures is MOST consistent with current at-risk-airway extubation guidance?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Extubate when the patient is fully awake, and position the AEC so its tip stays above the carina at approximately the same depth as the tracheal tube
choices.rationale: Correct. Guidelines for the at-risk airway recommend awake extubation, and the AEC should be left at the same depth as the tracheal tube with the tip kept above the carina to maintain a railroad for reintubation while avoiding deep placement that risks barotrauma.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Advance the AEC well past the carina into a mainstem bronchus to guarantee it cannot be dislodged, and extubate while deeply anesthetized
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Advancing the AEC beyond the carina (e.g., into a bronchus) and using it for oxygen insufflation at that depth is a recognized cause of barotrauma and pneumothorax; deep extubation is also not advised for an at-risk airway. Both elements are unsafe.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Extubate deep to avoid coughing on the tube, and remove the AEC immediately at extubation since edema will resolve quickly
choices.rationale: Incorrect. An at-risk airway should be extubated awake, not deep, and the AEC is intentionally left in place precisely because edema (and thus reintubation difficulty) persists; immediate removal discards the safety railroad.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Forgo the AEC entirely and instead pre-emptively perform a surgical tracheostomy in all such patients before extubation
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Routine tracheostomy is not the default strategy for every at-risk extubation; a staged AEC-assisted awake extubation is a less invasive, guideline-endorsed approach. Tracheostomy is reserved for selected cases, not mandated universally.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Keep the AEC in place but use it to deliver high-flow jet ventilation routinely while the patient breathes spontaneously
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Routine jet ventilation through an AEC is not standard and carries a significant barotrauma risk, particularly if the catheter tip is malpositioned. The catheter is primarily a conduit/guide for reintubation, not a routine ventilation device.

questions.explanation: When the airway is judged 'at risk' (difficult intubation, airway edema, distorted anatomy, or limited access), extubation should be planned and staged rather than treated as routine. Two anchored principles: (1) extubate the at-risk airway when the patient is FULLY AWAKE with restored airway reflexes; and (2) if using an airway exchange catheter to preserve a reintubation conduit, leave it at approximately the same depth as the tracheal tube with the tip ABOVE the carina. Keeping the tip above the carina, and avoiding routine oxygen insufflation/jet ventilation through it, is the key safety rule, because deep placement and pressurized insufflation through an AEC are well-documented causes of barotrauma and pneumothorax.

source.title: All India Difficult Airway Association 2025 guidelines for extubation of the 'at-risk' airway - PMC

verdict.reason:
URL loads, is public (PMC open-access article PMC12643154), and supports the keyed answer. The article on at-risk/difficult airway extubation confirms each load-bearing claim:

(1) Awake extubation for known difficult airway: "In instances involving a known difficult airway, it is advisable to extubate the child while they are awake." Supports option A's "extubate when fully awake."

(2) AEC positioning: the tip should "remain above the carina" and be placed at "the same depth as the tracheal tube." This matches option A almost verbatim.

(3) Distractors are refuted by the source and standard practice:
- B: advancing AEC into a mainstem bronchus risks trauma/bronchospasm and contradicts "tip to remain above the carina"; deep extubation is inappropriate for a known difficult airway. Wrong.
- C: deep extubation + immediate AEC removal defeats the rescue purpose of the AEC and is inappropriate for a difficult airway. Wrong.
- D: tracheostomy is described as the "last option," not a routine pre-emptive procedure in all such patients. Wrong.

The clinical vignette (8-hour prone anterior cervical fusion, Cormack-Lehane grade 3 difficult intubation, facial/airway edema, anticipated difficult reintubation) is an unambiguous at-risk-airway extubation scenario, and option A is the only choice consistent with current guidance (awake extubation + AEC tip above carina at ETT depth). Medicine is correct and unambiguous; rationales for correct and incorrect options are accurate. Safe to publish.

questions.stem: A healthy, muscular 24-year-old man undergoes uneventful knee arthroscopy. Immediately after extubation he develops laryngospasm with marked inspiratory effort against a closed glottis. The spasm is broken within about a minute with jaw thrust, CPAP, and a small dose of succinylcholine. Over the next several minutes he becomes hypoxemic, tachypneic, and coughs up pink, frothy secretions; his chest film shows bilateral diffuse infiltrates with a normal heart size. What is the most appropriate management of this complication?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Supportive care with supplemental oxygen and positive airway pressure (CPAP/PEEP); the condition is typically self-limited and resolves within 24-48 hours
choices.rationale: Correct. This is negative-pressure (post-obstructive) pulmonary edema. Management is supportive: maintain oxygenation with supplemental O2 and positive pressure (CPAP/NIV or PEEP if intubated). Most cases improve within 24-48 hours, and the majority can be managed without reintubation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Emergent thrombolysis for presumed pulmonary embolism
choices.rationale: Incorrect. The temporal link to laryngospasm against a closed glottis, pink frothy sputum, and bilateral infiltrates point to negative-pressure pulmonary edema, not pulmonary embolism. Thrombolysis is inappropriate and dangerous in the postoperative setting without evidence of PE.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Aggressive high-dose diuresis as the definitive first-line treatment to remove the alveolar fluid
choices.rationale: Incorrect. NPPE is not a volume-overload edema; the pulmonary capillary pressures and intravascular volume are typically normal. Diuretics are controversial and at best adjunctive in selected patients; oxygenation and positive airway pressure are the mainstay, not aggressive diuresis.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics and corticosteroids for aspiration pneumonitis
choices.rationale: Incorrect. The clinical picture is driven by negative intrathoracic pressure during obstruction, not aspiration. Routine antibiotics and steroids are not indicated; the cornerstone is supportive oxygenation and positive airway pressure.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Immediate cricothyrotomy because the edema indicates ongoing fixed upper-airway obstruction
choices.rationale: Incorrect. The laryngospasm has already been relieved; the problem is now lung edema, not a persistently obstructed upper airway. A surgical airway is not indicated and would not address the alveolar edema.

questions.explanation: Negative-pressure (post-obstructive) pulmonary edema classically follows acute upper-airway obstruction, most commonly post-extubation laryngospasm. Forceful inspiration against a closed glottis (a Mueller maneuver) generates markedly negative intrathoracic pressure (often -50 to -100 cm H2O), driving transudation of fluid into the alveoli. The typical patient is young, healthy, and muscular (often male, able to generate large negative pressures), and presents within minutes with hypoxemia, respiratory distress, and pink frothy secretions, with bilateral infiltrates and a normal heart size. Because it is a pressure-driven, not volume-overloaded, process, management is supportive: relieve the obstruction (already done here), maintain oxygenation, and apply positive airway pressure (CPAP/NIV, or PEEP if reintubated). Most cases resolve within 24-48 hours; diuretics are controversial and not the primary therapy.

Medicine is correct and unambiguous. The vignette is a textbook NPPE case: young muscular male generating strong negative intrathoracic pressure against a closed glottis after extubation, producing non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema. questions.stem: A healthy 5-year-old is undergoing bilateral strabismus repair under general anesthesia with a laryngeal mask airway and sevoflurane. As the surgeon places traction on the medial rectus muscle, the heart rate abruptly falls from 110 to 55 beats per minute with no change in rhythm morphology. Blood pressure is stable and SpO2 is 99%. What is the most appropriate FIRST step in management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ask the surgeon to immediately release traction on the extraocular muscle
choices.rationale: Correct. The oculocardiac reflex (trigeminal afferent, vagal efferent) is most often triggered by extraocular muscle traction, classically the medial rectus. Cessation of the triggering stimulus is the only definitive treatment, and the bradycardia typically resolves within 10-20 seconds once traction stops.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Administer IV atropine 0.02 mg/kg before doing anything else
choices.rationale: Incorrect as the first step. Anticholinergics are useful for recurrent or refractory episodes, but the immediate action is removal of the stimulus. Bradycardia from a single traction event usually self-resolves once the surgeon lets go, so reaching for a drug first is not the initial maneuver.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Deepen anesthesia with a propofol bolus to blunt the reflex
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Deepening anesthesia does not address the active vagal stimulus and a propofol bolus could worsen bradycardia and hypotension. The reflex arc is interrupted by stopping traction, not by adding hypnotic.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Begin chest compressions and call for the code cart
choices.rationale: Incorrect. The patient has a perfusing rhythm with stable blood pressure and oxygenation; this is sinus bradycardia, not arrest. Compressions are not indicated. Releasing traction is the appropriate response.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Administer IV ephedrine to raise the heart rate
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Ephedrine treats hypotension and has weak chronotropy, but it does not interrupt the vagally mediated reflex and ignores the obvious reversible cause. Stopping the traction is both faster and definitive.

questions.explanation: The oculocardiac reflex (Aschner reflex) is a trigeminovagal reflex: the afferent limb is the trigeminal nerve (CN V, ophthalmic division) and the efferent limb is the vagus nerve (CN X). It is most commonly provoked by traction on the extraocular muscles during strabismus surgery, with the medial rectus most frequently implicated, and presents as sudden sinus bradycardia. The single most important and only definitive treatment is immediate cessation of the triggering stimulus, so the first action is to ask the surgeon to release traction; the bradycardia usually resolves within seconds. The reflex is also fatigable. Anticholinergics (atropine or glycopyrrolate) are used for prophylaxis or for recurrent/severe episodes, but they are not the first step after a single traction-induced event.

It directly supports the answer: the source states "the only definitive treatment" is "immediate cessation of the triggering stimulus," and specifically names extraocular muscle traction (medial rectus) during strabismus surgery as a primary trigger causing bradycardia — exactly the stem scenario. Safe to publish.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old man presents for emergency repair of a penetrating open globe injury after being struck in the eye. He ate a full meal 45 minutes before the injury. He has no history of malignant hyperthermia, neuromuscular disease, or burns, and serum potassium is normal. You plan a rapid sequence induction. The team wishes to secure the airway quickly while minimizing any rise in intraocular pressure that could extrude ocular contents. Which neuromuscular blocking strategy is most appropriate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Rocuronium 1.2 mg/kg for rapid sequence induction
choices.rationale: Correct. A high-dose nondepolarizing relaxant such as rocuronium 1.2 mg/kg gives rapid, reliable intubating conditions for RSI without the transient IOP rise associated with succinylcholine, making it the preferred relaxant when avoiding any increase in IOP in an open globe with a full stomach.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Succinylcholine 1.5 mg/kg because its rapid onset is essential with a full stomach
choices.rationale: Incorrect in this scenario. Although succinylcholine provides the fastest onset and best intubating conditions, it produces a transient rise in IOP. With a high-dose nondepolarizing alternative available and a normal potassium, the strategy that avoids the IOP increase is preferred for an open globe.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Standard-dose vecuronium 0.1 mg/kg, accepting a 3-minute onset
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Vecuronium at intubating dose has too slow an onset for a true RSI in a full-stomach patient, prolonging the period of aspiration risk. A high-dose rocuronium RSI achieves comparable airway security far faster.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Awake fiberoptic intubation with topical anesthesia and no relaxant
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Coughing, bucking, and the Valsalva associated with awake intubation markedly raise IOP and can extrude globe contents, making this approach hazardous for an open globe injury.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Deep inhalational induction with sevoflurane and no neuromuscular blocker
choices.rationale: Incorrect. An unprotected inhalational induction is inappropriate for a full-stomach patient because it does not provide rapid airway protection against aspiration, and inadequate relaxation risks coughing that raises IOP.

questions.explanation: The open globe with a full stomach poses two competing priorities: protect the airway rapidly against aspiration and avoid any rise in intraocular pressure that could extrude vitreous. Rapid sequence induction is the preferred technique. Succinylcholine offers the fastest onset and best intubating conditions but causes a transient increase in IOP, so when the patient has no contraindication to a nondepolarizer and normal potassium, a high-dose nondepolarizing relaxant such as rocuronium 1.2 mg/kg is favored: it produces rapid, reliable intubating conditions for RSI without the IOP rise. Rocuronium's only absolute contraindication is allergy, and its prolonged block can be reversed with sugammadex if needed. Slow-onset relaxants and unprotected inhalational or awake techniques either expose the patient to aspiration or risk coughing-induced IOP spikes.

source.title: Rocuronium versus succinylcholine for rapid sequence induction intubation - PMC (Cochrane review)

verdict.reason:
VERIFIED — publishable.

SOURCE: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7104695/ loads, is public (PMC open-access Cochrane review "Rocuronium versus succinylcholine for rapid sequence induction intubation"). It supports the load-bearing facts: (1) succinylcholine is associated with increases in intraocular pressure; (2) high-dose rocuronium 1.2 mg/kg provides intubating conditions with no statistically significant difference from succinylcholine (i.e., RSI-adequate rapid onset, ~60s); (3) rocuronium can be reversed with sugammadex. Note: the source does not explicitly discuss open globe injury and its headline favors succinylcholine for general RSI, but it is supportive (not contradictory) of every premise the answer depends on.

MEDICINE: Correct and unambiguous. This is a classic anesthesiology scenario — penetrating open globe injury + full stomach (45 min, aspiration risk) mandates RSI while avoiding any IOP rise that could extrude ocular contents. Succinylcholine's transient IOP increase makes it the hazard to avoid; high-dose rocuronium (1.2 mg/kg) gives rapid onset without raising IOP, making A the most appropriate choice. The patient has no succinylcholine contraindications listed (no MH, neuromuscular disease, burns, normal K+) — this is deliberate, so the distinguishing factor is the IOP concern, not an absolute contraindication, and A remains correct.

RATIONALES/DISTRACTORS all accurate: B (succinylcholine) is the intended trap — rapid onset is real but the IOP rise is specifically dangerous in open globe. C (vecuronium 0.1 mg/kg) — 3-min onset too slow for RSI. D (awake fiberoptic) — coughing/straining raises IOP, impractical/unsafe with open globe. E (deep inhalational, no relaxant) — inadequate for RSI with full stomach, aspiration risk and poor intubating conditions. Single best answer is clear.

questions.stem: You are planning the anesthetic for a patient with a ruptured open globe in whom maintaining a low intraocular pressure is critical. While reviewing your induction agents, you want to choose drugs that lower or do not raise IOP. Which of the following agents is most likely to INCREASE intraocular pressure and should therefore be used with caution?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Succinylcholine
choices.rationale: Correct. Succinylcholine is classically associated with a transient increase in IOP and is grouped with ketamine as an IOP-raising agent in standard references, which is why it is approached cautiously in open globe injury.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Propofol
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Propofol is a hypnotic induction agent that lowers IOP (most induction agents decrease IOP by roughly 30%), making it desirable for open globe cases.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sevoflurane
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Volatile anesthetics such as sevoflurane decrease IOP in a dose-dependent fashion and do not raise it.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fentanyl
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Opioids such as fentanyl lower IOP and also blunt the IOP response to laryngoscopy, so they are helpful rather than harmful in this setting.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Thiopental
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Thiopental, like propofol and etomidate, decreases IOP by approximately 30% and is not an IOP-raising agent.

questions.explanation: When intraocular pressure must be kept low (as in an open globe), the anesthesiologist should understand which agents raise versus lower IOP. Most intravenous induction agents (propofol, thiopental, etomidate) decrease IOP by approximately 30%, volatile anesthetics decrease IOP, and opioids such as fentanyl lower IOP while blunting the laryngoscopy response. In contrast, increases in IOP are classically associated with succinylcholine and ketamine. Succinylcholine produces a transient rise in IOP, which is the basis for caution in open globe injury; therefore among the listed options it is the agent most likely to increase IOP. (Note: etomidate may also indirectly raise IOP via myoclonus, but it is not among the answer options here.)

source.title: Anesthesia for Ophthalmic Surgery - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf

verdict.reason: URL loads and is public (NCBI Bookshelf / StatPearls NBK572131). The source directly supports the answer: it states "Increases in IOP can be seen with drugs like succinylcholine" and recommends avoiding depolarizing agents due to risk of transient IOP increase, confirming A (succinylcholine) as the agent most likely to INCREASE intraocular pressure. All distractors are correctly non-raising per the source: "Most induction agents, including propofol, thiopental, and etomidate, decrease IOP by approximately 30%" (covers B propofol and E thiopental); volatile anesthetics including sevoflurane decrease IOP (C); and opioids/fentanyl attenuate the IOP rise from laryngoscopy (D). The medicine is correct and unambiguous with a single best answer, and all option rationales are accurate. The low-IOP/open-globe framing in the stem is standard, accurate clinical context; the core pharmacology tested is fully supported by the cited source.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman who has never smoked is scheduled for outpatient laparoscopic cholecystectomy under general anesthesia. She reports severe motion sickness on car trips and vomited after a previous wisdom-tooth extraction. The anesthetic plan includes postoperative oral oxycodone for discharge analgesia. Using the Apfel simplified risk score, which strategy best matches her predicted risk of postoperative nausea and vomiting (PONV)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: She has an Apfel score of 4; use a total intravenous (propofol) technique plus combination prophylaxis with multiple antiemetics from different classes
choices.rationale: Female sex, nonsmoking status, history of PONV/motion sickness, and anticipated postoperative opioids are all four Apfel risk factors, giving a score of 4 (~80% baseline PONV risk). The highest-yield approach is a baseline-risk-reduction technique (TIVA with propofol, avoiding volatile agents and nitrous oxide) combined with multiple antiemetics from different mechanistic classes.

choices.label: B
choices.text: She has an Apfel score of 2; a single antiemetic agent is sufficient
choices.rationale: She actually meets all four Apfel criteria, not two. Undercounting her risk and giving only a single agent would leave a high-risk patient substantially under-prophylaxed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: She has an Apfel score of 4, but prophylaxis should be withheld and antiemetics reserved for rescue treatment only
choices.rationale: The score is correct, but a rescue-only strategy is inappropriate for a high-risk patient. Guidelines recommend multimodal prophylaxis for scores of 3-4; reserving treatment for rescue accepts an ~80% PONV rate.

choices.label: D
choices.text: She has an Apfel score of 1; no intervention is warranted because young women rarely experience PONV
choices.rationale: This both miscounts the score and contradicts the data: young, nonsmoking women with a PONV/motion-sickness history are among the highest-risk patients, not the lowest.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Her risk cannot be estimated preoperatively because the Apfel score requires intraoperative volatile-agent dose
choices.rationale: The simplified Apfel score uses only four readily available factors (female sex, nonsmoker, PONV/motion-sickness history, postoperative opioids) and is specifically designed for preoperative risk stratification; volatile dose is not one of its components.

questions.explanation: The simplified Apfel score assigns one point each for female sex, nonsmoking status, history of PONV or motion sickness, and anticipated use of postoperative opioids. Predicted PONV incidence rises roughly 10% -> 20% -> 40% -> 60% -> 80% as the score climbs from 0 to 4. This patient scores 4. For high scores (3-4), the evidence-based approach is to lower baseline risk (total intravenous anesthesia with propofol, avoiding inhalational agents and nitrous oxide, minimizing opioids) AND administer combination prophylaxis using several antiemetics from different classes, because each single agent confers only about a 25% relative risk reduction. A single agent, rescue-only dosing, or no prophylaxis all leave this patient inadequately covered.

source.title: Postoperative Nausea - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf

verdict.reason:
VERIFIED — publish. Source https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK500029/ loads successfully, is public (NCBI Bookshelf / StatPearls, no paywall or auth), and supports the keyed answer.

Clinical correctness: The patient has all four Apfel simplified risk-score factors — (1) female sex, (2) non-smoker ("never smoked"), (3) history of PONV/motion sickness (severe car motion sickness plus prior post-extraction vomiting), and (4) planned postoperative opioids (oral oxycodone). Four factors = Apfel score of 4, the highest tier (~80% PONV risk). The source explicitly states "having 4 risk factors may correlate to approximately 80% chance of PONV."

Answer A is correct and unambiguous: high-risk patients warrant a propofol-based total intravenous technique (avoiding volatile agents) plus combination prophylaxis with multiple antiemetic classes. The source supports each element: "Propofol... is useful in high-risk patients as part of a total intravenous anesthetic (avoidance of inhalational agents)"; "treatment of PONV is most effective with a combination of agents"; "some algorithms would recommend 4 antiemetic agents be used prophylactically"; and "prevention is more effective than initiating therapy after the onset of symptoms."

Distractor rationales are accurate: B (score 2) undercounts her factors; C (withhold prophylaxis, rescue only) contradicts the prevention-over-rescue principle; D (score 1, no intervention) is factually and clinically wrong; E is a correct trap — the SIMPLIFIED Apfel score uses only the four preoperative factors and does NOT require an intraoperative volatile-agent dose (that belongs to other models such as Koivuranta/Apfel-extended), so risk is fully estimable preoperatively.

Minor note (not disqualifying): the source does not spell out the full 10/20/40/60/80% gradient for scores 0-3, but it does anchor the score-of-4 ≈ 80% figure, and answer A only requires recognizing the score of 4 plus the TIVA + combination-prophylaxis strategy, all of which are directly supported. The Apfel score is a standard, well-established anesthesiology tool and the question's framing is accurate.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man is evaluated before an outpatient inguinal hernia repair. He reports loud snoring, witnessed apneas, and daytime tiredness. He is hypertensive, BMI 36 kg/m2, neck circumference 44 cm. He has never had a sleep study. Which assessment most appropriately guides his perioperative risk for this same-day surgery?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Calculate a STOP-BANG score; he meets at least 6 of 8 criteria, placing him at high risk for OSA and warranting heightened perioperative vigilance and opioid-sparing planning
choices.rationale: STOP-BANG screens Snoring, Tiredness, Observed apnea, high blood Pressure, BMI >35, Age >50, Neck >40 cm, and male Gender. This patient has snoring, tiredness, observed apnea, hypertension, BMI >35, age >50, neck >40 cm, and male sex (8/8), placing him at high risk. A high score triggers OSA-conscious planning: opioid minimization, regional/multimodal analgesia, and enhanced postoperative monitoring.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Surgery should be canceled outright because any unconfirmed OSA is an absolute contraindication to ambulatory surgery
choices.rationale: Suspected or even known OSA is not an absolute contraindication. With appropriate procedure selection, opioid-sparing technique, and monitoring, many such patients can safely undergo ambulatory surgery; routine cancellation is not supported.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A polysomnogram is mandatory before any ambulatory procedure can proceed in a patient with suspected OSA
choices.rationale: A formal sleep study is not required before proceeding. Validated screening (STOP-BANG) lets the team treat the patient as presumed-OSA and manage accordingly without delaying surgery for polysomnography.

choices.label: D
choices.text: OSA risk is irrelevant for this case because hernia repair is a low-risk, short procedure
choices.rationale: Procedure brevity does not eliminate OSA-related risk; sedatives, opioids, and residual anesthetic depress airway tone and ventilation regardless of case length, so screening and tailored planning still matter.

choices.label: E
choices.text: His Apfel score should be used to quantify his airway and respiratory risk from OSA
choices.rationale: The Apfel score predicts postoperative nausea and vomiting, not OSA or perioperative respiratory risk. STOP-BANG is the appropriate OSA screening tool.

questions.explanation: STOP-BANG is the recommended bedside screen for OSA in surgical patients; the acronym captures Snoring, Tiredness, Observed apnea, blood Pressure, BMI >35 kg/m2, Age >50 yr, Neck circumference >40 cm, and male Gender. A score of 3 or more indicates intermediate-to-high risk, and 5-8 indicates high risk. This patient meets all eight criteria. Suspected OSA does not mandate cancellation or a preoperative sleep study; rather, the patient is managed as presumed-OSA with optimized comorbidities, opioid-sparing multimodal/regional analgesia, and heightened postoperative respiratory vigilance. Patients with OSA do carry roughly two-fold higher odds of same-day admission, so the team must be prepared for escalation.

source.title: Safety considerations with the current ambulatory trends: more complicated procedures and more complicated patients - PMC

verdict.reason: URL loads, is public, and supports the answer. The source ("Safety considerations with the current ambulatory trends," PMC10562071) explicitly endorses the validated STOP-BANG questionnaire to screen for OSA risk in ambulatory/sedation patients and frames OSA screening + optimization as part of patient selection rather than a barrier to ambulatory surgery. Medicine is correct and unambiguous: (A) is the best answer. Patient meets all 8 STOP-BANG criteria — Snoring, Tiredness, Observed apnea, Pressure/HTN, BMI >35 (36), Age >50 (58), Neck >40cm (44), male Gender — so "at least 6 of 8" is true and he is high-risk, warranting heightened vigilance and opioid-sparing planning. Distractors are clearly wrong: (B) suspected OSA is not an absolute contraindication to ambulatory surgery; (C) STOP-BANG screening exists precisely because PSG is not mandatory before ambulatory procedures; (D) OSA risk is relevant; (E) the Apfel score quantifies PONV risk, not airway/respiratory/OSA risk — a correct trap. Rationale is accurate. Safe to publish.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman is recovering in the PACU after outpatient knee arthroscopy. The team is using validated scoring systems to guide her recovery. Which statement correctly reflects how the Aldrete and Post-Anesthesia Discharge Scoring System (PADSS) tools are applied during her recovery?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The Aldrete score guides Phase I discharge from the PACU (a score of 8 or higher indicates readiness), while a PADSS-type system assesses readiness for discharge home from Phase II
choices.rationale: The modified Aldrete score evaluates Phase I PACU recovery (activity, respiration, circulation, consciousness, oxygen saturation), with a score of 8 or more indicating suitability for PACU discharge. PADSS, which assesses vital signs, ambulation, nausea/vomiting, pain, and surgical bleeding, is used to determine home-readiness from Phase II.

choices.label: B
choices.text: An Aldrete score of 8 or higher is the sole criterion required to discharge the patient directly home after ambulatory surgery
choices.rationale: The Aldrete score addresses Phase I PACU readiness, not street/home-readiness. Home discharge after outpatient surgery additionally requires assessment of ambulation, pain, nausea/vomiting, bleeding, and a responsible escort, captured by a PADSS-type system.

choices.label: C
choices.text: PADSS is used to determine Phase I PACU discharge, and the Aldrete score determines home-readiness
choices.rationale: This reverses the two tools. Aldrete is applied to Phase I PACU recovery; PADSS is applied to Phase II home-readiness.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Both scores require documented oral intake and successful voiding before any patient may leave the PACU
choices.rationale: Mandatory drinking and voiding were removed from the modified PADSS for average-risk patients because they delayed discharge without improving outcomes; they are not universal prerequisites, and the Aldrete score never included them.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The Aldrete score must reach 18 or higher before a patient is fit for Phase I PACU discharge
choices.rationale: The Phase I modified Aldrete threshold is 8 or more (out of 10). A threshold of 18 corresponds to the modified Aldrete score used for ambulatory/home-readiness in some institutions, not Phase I PACU discharge.

questions.explanation: Ambulatory recovery is staged. The modified Aldrete score grades Phase I PACU recovery across five domains (activity, respiration, circulation, consciousness, oxygen saturation), and a score of 8 or higher (of 10) indicates the patient is suitable for discharge from the PACU. Home-readiness from Phase II is judged by a PADSS-type system covering vital signs, ambulation, nausea/vomiting, pain, and surgical bleeding. The modified PADSS dropped mandatory oral intake and voiding for average-risk outpatients because those requirements delayed discharge without improving safety. Confusing which tool governs which phase, or treating Phase I readiness as equivalent to home discharge, is a common error.

source.title: Aldrete Scoring System - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf

verdict.reason: URL loads and is public (NCBI Bookshelf/StatPearls NBK594237). Source directly supports correct answer A: it states "the modified Aldrete scoring system and the PADSS are frequently used in the PACU to assist decision-making for Phase I discharge and discharge from Phase II to home, respectively," and that an Aldrete score "8 or higher indicates that a patient is suitable for discharge from the PACU." Medicine is correct and unambiguous. All distractors are accurately wrong: (B) Aldrete is Phase I, not the sole criterion for direct home discharge; (C) reverses the two tools; (D) oral intake/voiding are not mandatory criteria (modern PADSS made these optional and Aldrete does not include them); (E) max Aldrete score is 10, not 18, and threshold is 8. Question, correct answer, and rationales are all sound for a paid bank.

questions.stem: An ambulatory surgery center is reviewing four patients scheduled for elective outpatient procedures. Which patient is the LEAST appropriate candidate for same-day surgery and discharge as currently presented?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A 72-year-old man, ASA III with well-controlled hypertension and stable coronary disease, undergoing cataract surgery, with his daughter driving him home
choices.rationale: Advanced age alone is not a contraindication. A stable ASA III patient with optimized comorbidities undergoing a low-risk procedure, with a responsible escort, is an acceptable ambulatory candidate.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A 50-year-old woman, ASA II with diet-controlled diabetes, undergoing hand surgery under regional block, with a friend to accompany her home
choices.rationale: A stable ASA II patient with a well-controlled comorbidity, a low-risk procedure, regional anesthesia, and an escort is a good ambulatory candidate.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A 60-year-old man with poorly controlled heart failure and worsening dyspnea at rest over the past week, scheduled for outpatient hernia repair, planning to drive himself home
choices.rationale: Acutely decompensated, unstable systemic disease (worsening heart failure with new rest dyspnea) is not optimized and warrants evaluation and stabilization before elective surgery. He also lacks a responsible adult escort, which is itself a barrier to same-day discharge. Both features make him the least appropriate candidate.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A 40-year-old woman, ASA II, obese with CPAP-compliant obstructive sleep apnea, undergoing knee arthroscopy with an opioid-sparing multimodal plan and a spouse to drive her home
choices.rationale: CPAP-adherent OSA with optimized comorbidities, an opioid-sparing analgesic plan, and a responsible escort is acceptable for ambulatory surgery; OSA itself is not an absolute exclusion.

choices.label: E
choices.text: A 35-year-old healthy man, ASA I, undergoing outpatient inguinal hernia repair with a coworker arranged to drive him home
choices.rationale: A healthy ASA I patient undergoing a low-risk procedure with a confirmed responsible escort is an ideal ambulatory candidate.

questions.explanation: Sound outpatient selection turns on disease stability and optimization rather than ASA number or age per se: stable ASA III patients (and stable ASA IV patients for low-risk procedures) can be appropriate, and advanced age alone is not a contraindication. Two red flags disqualify patient C: (1) acutely decompensated, unoptimized systemic disease (worsening heart failure with new dyspnea at rest), which should be evaluated and stabilized before elective surgery, and (2) the absence of a responsible adult to escort and care for him after anesthesia, a near-universal requirement for same-day discharge. The other patients have stable, optimized comorbidities, low-risk procedures, and confirmed escorts.

source.title: Safety considerations with the current ambulatory trends: more complicated procedures and more complicated patients - PMC

verdict.reason: URL loads, is public (PMC10562071), and supports the answer. The article on ambulatory surgery patient selection states ASA III patients who are STABLE are acceptable and that facilities should exclude/avoid sicker, unstable patients lacking resources for higher-acuity care. Option C is unambiguously correct and least appropriate on two independent grounds: (1) poorly controlled heart failure with worsening dyspnea at rest is decompensated/unstable disease (NYHA class IV) requiring optimization before any elective surgery — not a stable candidate; (2) planning to drive himself home violates the universal requirement for a responsible adult escort after anesthesia/sedation. Distractors are all appropriate: A is STABLE ASA III with stable CAD/controlled HTN for low-risk cataract surgery with a driver; B has diet-controlled diabetes, regional block, and an escort; D has CPAP-compliant (optimized) OSA with an opioid-sparing multimodal plan and a driver — textbook-appropriate; E is ASA I healthy with a driver. Medicine is correct, unambiguous, and rationales are accurate. Safe to publish.

questions.stem: A 64-year-old man with type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 7.4%) is scheduled for an elective right total knee arthroplasty at 0730. His home regimen is metformin 1000 mg twice daily, empagliflozin 10 mg daily, and insulin glargine 30 units at bedtime. He is otherwise healthy with an eGFR of 78 mL/min. At his preoperative visit two weeks before surgery, which instruction is the MOST appropriate component of his medication plan?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Continue metformin through the morning of surgery and stop the empagliflozin only on the day of surgery
choices.rationale: Metformin is held on the day of surgery because of the theoretical risk of lactic acidosis with renal hypoperfusion, and SGLT-2 inhibitors require cessation 24-72 hours before surgery, not the morning of, because of euglycemic ketoacidosis risk. This option reverses both recommendations.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Stop the empagliflozin 3 days before surgery, hold metformin on the day of surgery, and reduce the bedtime glargine to about 75-80% of the usual dose the night before
choices.rationale: Correct. SGLT-2 inhibitors should be discontinued 24-72 hours preoperatively to avoid euglycemic DKA, metformin is held on the day of surgery, and basal insulin is reduced (roughly 20-25%, i.e., given at 75-80% of the usual dose) the evening before to prevent fasting hypoglycemia while avoiding rebound hyperglycemia.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Continue the full bedtime dose of glargine the night before surgery to prevent rebound hyperglycemia
choices.rationale: Giving the full basal dose the night before a fast increases the risk of hypoglycemia. Guidance is to reduce the long-acting basal dose (commonly by 20-25%, more in patients at high hypoglycemia risk), not to continue it unchanged.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hold all insulin entirely from midnight before surgery and rely on a sliding scale
choices.rationale: Completely omitting basal insulin in a patient with diabetes risks marked hyperglycemia and, in insulin-deficient patients, ketoacidosis. Basal insulin is reduced rather than fully withheld; correctional (sliding-scale) insulin supplements but does not replace basal coverage.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Target an intraoperative blood glucose of 110-140 mg/dL with a tight insulin protocol
choices.rationale: Overly tight glycemic targets increase hypoglycemia risk without outcome benefit. The recommended perioperative target is approximately 140-180 mg/dL (ADA accepts 80-180 mg/dL), not 110-140 mg/dL.

questions.explanation: Perioperative glycemic management hinges on agent-specific timing. SGLT-2 inhibitors (e.g., empagliflozin) must be stopped 24-72 hours before surgery because they can precipitate euglycemic diabetic ketoacidosis in fasting or stressed patients—the normal glucose masks the ketoacidosis. Metformin is held on the day of surgery given lactic-acidosis concerns with potential renal hypoperfusion. Basal insulin is reduced (typically 20-25%, so about 75-80% of the usual dose, and 50-75% reduction in patients at high hypoglycemia risk) the evening before, rather than continued in full or omitted entirely, to balance hypoglycemia against hyperglycemia/ketosis. The accepted perioperative glucose target is roughly 140-180 mg/dL, avoiding the harms of overly tight control.

source.title: Diabetic Perioperative Management - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf

verdict.reason: URL loads publicly (NCBI StatPearls Bookshelf, NBK540965, "Perioperative Management of the Diabetic Patient"). It directly supports answer B on all three components: (1) SGLT2 inhibitors/empagliflozin should be stopped 24-72 hours before surgery due to euglycemic DKA risk — option B's "stop 3 days before" (= 72 h) is within and consistent with this conservative recommendation; (2) metformin should be discontinued on the day of surgery (lactic acidosis concern) — matches B; (3) long-acting basal insulin (glargine) should be reduced 20-25% the evening before surgery — "reduce to about 75-80% of usual dose" is exactly a 20-25% reduction. The distractors are all correctly wrong: A contradicts holding metformin and stopping SGLT2i early; C contradicts the basal-dose reduction; D (holding all insulin + sliding scale) risks hyperglycemia/DKA since basal should be continued at reduced dose; E proposes overly tight intraoperative glucose targets (110-140) which is not recommended (looser 140-180 targets preferred to avoid hypoglycemia). Medicine is correct and unambiguous; eGFR 78 supports safe metformin use until day of surgery; rationales are accurate. Safe to publish.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman with poorly controlled Graves disease presents 12 hours after an emergency appendectomy with temperature 40.1°C, heart rate 165 (atrial fibrillation), agitation, and a markedly elevated free T4. Thyroid storm is diagnosed. After supportive measures, intravenous propranolol and hydrocortisone are given and propylthiouracil (PTU) is started. The team prepares to add saturated solution of potassium iodide (SSKI). Regarding the iodide, which action is MOST appropriate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Administer SSKI approximately one hour after the PTU dose
choices.rationale: Correct. The thionamide (PTU) is given first to block new hormone synthesis; iodide is then delayed about one hour so that the iodine load does not transiently fuel additional hormone synthesis before the synthesis blockade is established. Iodide subsequently inhibits hormone release (Wolff-Chaikoff effect).

choices.label: B
choices.text: Administer SSKI immediately, before or simultaneously with the PTU, to block hormone release fastest
choices.rationale: Giving iodide before or with the thionamide can paradoxically increase thyroid hormone synthesis by providing substrate before synthesis is blocked, potentially worsening the storm. The thionamide must precede iodide.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Omit iodide entirely because beta-blockade and PTU are sufficient
choices.rationale: Iodide is a recommended component of thyroid storm therapy because it rapidly inhibits release of preformed hormone. It should be added (after the thionamide), not omitted, in true storm.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Give iodide first and delay PTU until thyroid function tests normalize
choices.rationale: This is the dangerous reverse sequence: iodide before the synthesis blocker can augment hormone production, and delaying PTU removes the synthesis blockade entirely. PTU must be started promptly and before iodide.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Replace PTU with iodide because PTU does not block peripheral T4-to-T3 conversion
choices.rationale: This is factually wrong: PTU is actually favored in storm precisely because, unlike methimazole, it additionally blocks peripheral conversion of T4 to T3. Iodide does not substitute for a thionamide's synthesis blockade.

questions.explanation: Thyroid storm therapy follows a deliberate sequence targeting hormone synthesis, release, peripheral conversion, and adrenergic effects. A thionamide (PTU or methimazole) is given to block new synthesis; PTU is preferred in storm because it also blocks peripheral T4-to-T3 conversion. Iodide (SSKI/Lugol's) blocks release of preformed hormone, but it must be given roughly one hour AFTER the thionamide. Administering iodine before synthesis is blocked supplies substrate that can transiently increase hormone production and worsen the crisis. Beta-blockade (propranolol) controls adrenergic symptoms and also blunts peripheral conversion, and glucocorticoids treat relative adrenal insufficiency and reduce conversion.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman with primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison disease) on hydrocortisone 20 mg/day and fludrocortisone undergoes an emergency exploratory laparotomy for bowel obstruction. Two hours postoperatively she becomes hypotensive (78/44 mmHg) and poorly responsive to fluids and vasopressors, with serum sodium 128 mEq/L and potassium 5.9 mEq/L. Which intervention is MOST appropriate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Administer 100 mg IV hydrocortisone now, followed by 200 mg over the next 24 hours, with aggressive isotonic fluid resuscitation
choices.rationale: Correct. This is acute adrenal crisis in a patient with primary adrenal insufficiency undergoing major surgical stress. Immediate parenteral hydrocortisone (100 mg bolus then ~200 mg/24 h, often as continuous infusion) plus IV fluid resuscitation is the standard treatment. The high-dose hydrocortisone also provides adequate mineralocorticoid activity.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Give IV dexamethasone 8 mg and withhold further steroids pending a random cortisol level
choices.rationale: Dexamethasone lacks meaningful mineralocorticoid activity, which this hyponatremic, hyperkalemic patient needs, and delaying definitive treatment to await a cortisol level is unsafe in an evolving crisis. Hydrocortisone is the agent of choice and treatment should not be delayed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Continue only her usual oral hydrocortisone 20 mg/day because she already takes a maintenance steroid
choices.rationale: Maintenance dosing is grossly inadequate for major surgical stress and active crisis. Patients with primary adrenal insufficiency require stress-dose parenteral glucocorticoids for major surgery and crisis, far exceeding the home maintenance dose.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Administer additional fludrocortisone orally to correct the hyperkalemia and hyponatremia
choices.rationale: Oral fludrocortisone has slow onset and is inappropriate for an acute crisis with hemodynamic instability; moreover, high-dose IV hydrocortisone itself confers sufficient mineralocorticoid effect acutely. Parenteral hydrocortisone and fluids are required.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Withhold steroids because current guidelines show stress-dose steroids are unnecessary in adrenal insufficiency
choices.rationale: The de-emphasis of routine stress dosing applies chiefly to secondary adrenal insufficiency for minor procedures. Primary adrenal insufficiency, especially with major surgery and an active crisis, always requires supplemental hydrocortisone. Withholding steroids here is dangerous.

questions.explanation: This is acute adrenal crisis: refractory hypotension plus hyponatremia and hyperkalemia (reflecting the aldosterone deficiency characteristic of PRIMARY adrenal insufficiency) in a patient under major surgical stress. The treatment is immediate IV hydrocortisone—100 mg bolus followed by about 200 mg over 24 hours (continuous infusion is superior to boluses)—together with aggressive isotonic fluid resuscitation. Hydrocortisone is chosen over dexamethasone because at these doses it supplies needed mineralocorticoid activity. While recent literature questions routine stress-dose steroids in secondary adrenal insufficiency for minor procedures, primary adrenal insufficiency with major surgery and crisis always requires supplementation, and treatment must never be delayed to await laboratory confirmation.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with episodic headaches, palpitations, and diaphoresis is found to have a 4-cm adrenal mass and markedly elevated plasma metanephrines, consistent with pheochromocytoma. He is scheduled for laparoscopic adrenalectomy. His resting heart rate is 110 with frequent palpitations, and the surgical team asks how to begin preoperative pharmacologic preparation. Which approach is MOST appropriate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Start a beta-blocker (metoprolol) first to control the tachycardia, then add alpha-blockade closer to surgery
choices.rationale: Starting beta-blockade before adequate alpha-blockade is dangerous: it removes beta-2-mediated vasodilation and leaves unopposed alpha-mediated vasoconstriction, which can precipitate hypertensive crisis or pulmonary edema. Alpha-blockade must come first.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Begin alpha-blockade (e.g., phenoxybenzamine) at least about 2 weeks before surgery, then add a beta-blocker only after adequate alpha-blockade if tachycardia persists
choices.rationale: Correct. Alpha-blockade is established first, started at least roughly 2 weeks preoperatively to allow full blockade and restoration of intravascular volume. A beta-blocker is added only afterward, once alpha-blockade is adequate, to control reflex or persistent tachycardia.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Use beta-blockade alone, since it controls both the hypertension and the tachycardia of catecholamine excess
choices.rationale: Beta-blockade alone does not control catecholamine-driven hypertension and, given without alpha-blockade, can worsen it through unopposed alpha stimulation. Alpha-blockade is the foundation of preparation; beta-blockade is adjunctive.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Proceed directly to surgery without blockade because the tumor is small and will be removed laparoscopically
choices.rationale: Tumor size and surgical approach do not eliminate the risk of intraoperative catecholamine surges. Adequate preoperative alpha-blockade (begun about 2 weeks ahead) dramatically reduces perioperative mortality and is required regardless of size.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Start alpha- and beta-blockade simultaneously on the same day, two days before surgery
choices.rationale: Two days is insufficient time to achieve full alpha-blockade and restore blood volume (about 2 weeks is needed), and beta-blockade should follow established alpha-blockade rather than begin concurrently from the outset.

questions.explanation: The cardinal rule of pheochromocytoma preparation is alpha-blockade BEFORE beta-blockade. Alpha-blockade (classically phenoxybenzamine, a non-selective alpha-blocker; selective alpha-1 agents and calcium channel blockers are increasingly used) is started at least about 2 weeks preoperatively to achieve full receptor blockade and allow gradual restoration of the contracted intravascular volume. Only after adequate alpha-blockade is a beta-blocker added to control reflex tachycardia or arrhythmia. Initiating beta-blockade first removes beta-2-mediated vasodilation, leaving unopposed alpha-mediated vasoconstriction that can trigger hypertensive crisis or pulmonary edema. Adequate preparation markedly reduces perioperative mortality.

The correct answer B is the single best choice. Safe to publish.

questions.stem: A 4-day-old neonate is brought to the OR for an emergent procedure. The anesthetist reviews the infant's oxygen transport physiology and notes that fetal hemoglobin (HbF) still predominates. Compared with adult hemoglobin (HbA), HbF shifts the oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve and changes the P50. Which single statement BEST describes the effect of fetal hemoglobin and its mechanism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: HbF shifts the curve to the LEFT and lowers the P50 because it binds 2,3-DPG poorly, increasing oxygen affinity
choices.rationale: Correct. HbF has reduced binding of 2,3-DPG (its gamma chains interact poorly with 2,3-DPG compared with the beta chains of HbA), so its oxygen affinity is higher. This produces a leftward shift and a lower P50, favoring oxygen uptake from the maternal circulation across the placenta.

choices.label: B
choices.text: HbF shifts the curve to the RIGHT and raises the P50 because it offloads oxygen more readily to fetal tissues
choices.rationale: Incorrect. A rightward shift with a higher P50 describes decreased affinity. HbF actually has INCREASED affinity (left shift, lower P50); its high affinity favors oxygen loading from the placenta, not enhanced peripheral offloading.

choices.label: C
choices.text: HbF shifts the curve to the LEFT because it binds 2,3-DPG more avidly than HbA, raising the P50
choices.rationale: Incorrect and internally contradictory. The direction (left) is right, but the mechanism is wrong: HbF binds 2,3-DPG LESS avidly, and a left shift corresponds to a LOWER P50, not a raised one.

choices.label: D
choices.text: HbF has no effect on the position of the curve because P50 is determined solely by pH and temperature
choices.rationale: Incorrect. P50 is influenced by pH, temperature, CO2, 2,3-DPG, and the hemoglobin species itself. HbF intrinsically shifts the curve left independent of pH and temperature.

choices.label: E
choices.text: HbF shifts the curve to the RIGHT because lower neonatal temperature increases 2,3-DPG binding
choices.rationale: Incorrect on multiple counts: lower temperature shifts the curve LEFT (not right), and the defining feature of HbF is reduced 2,3-DPG interaction, not increased binding.

questions.explanation: The oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve position is summarized by the P50 (the PaO2 at which hemoglobin is 50% saturated; normal adult ~26-27 mmHg). A LEFT shift = increased affinity = lower P50; a RIGHT shift = decreased affinity = higher P50. Classic left-shift causes: alkalosis (high pH), hypothermia, decreased CO2, decreased 2,3-DPG, carboxyhemoglobin, methemoglobin, and fetal hemoglobin. HbF's high affinity arises because its gamma subunits bind 2,3-DPG poorly, so 2,3-DPG cannot stabilize the deoxygenated (T) state as effectively. The resulting leftward shift lets the fetus extract oxygen from maternal blood across the placenta. Remembering that anything reducing 2,3-DPG (stored blood, HbF, alkalosis) shifts the curve left is a high-yield board concept.

Distractors are all clearly wrong: B reverses curve direction and physiology; C is internally contradictory (left shift yet "raising P50") and falsely claims avid DPG binding; D wrongly excludes affinity/DPG factors; E invents a false temperature/2,3-DPG mechanism. Rationale for correct choice is accurate. Note: the source does not quote a numeric P50 value, but the lower-P50 inference is the direct definitional consequence of the explicitly stated left shift/higher affinity, so the answer remains sound. Safe to publish.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with diabetic ketoacidosis has an arterial blood gas showing pH 7.22, PaCO2 30 mmHg, and HCO3- 12 mEq/L. To determine whether his respiratory response is appropriate or whether a second acid-base disturbance is present, you apply Winter's formula. The expected PaCO2 is (1.5 x HCO3-) + 8 ± 2. What is the BEST interpretation of his measured PaCO2 of 30 mmHg?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Expected PaCO2 is 24-28 mmHg; the measured PaCO2 of 30 is higher than expected, indicating a superimposed respiratory acidosis (inadequate compensation)
choices.rationale: Correct. Expected PaCO2 = (1.5 x 12) + 8 ± 2 = 18 + 8 = 26 ± 2 = 24-28 mmHg. The measured 30 mmHg exceeds the upper bound, so the lungs are not blowing off enough CO2 - a concurrent respiratory acidosis is present on top of the metabolic acidosis.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Expected PaCO2 is 24-28 mmHg; the measured PaCO2 of 30 falls within range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation only
choices.rationale: Incorrect. The calculated range is 24-28 mmHg, and 30 lies ABOVE it, so the compensation is not within range. This option misreads the boundary.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Expected PaCO2 is 24-28 mmHg; the measured PaCO2 of 30 is lower than expected, indicating a superimposed respiratory alkalosis
choices.rationale: Incorrect. A PaCO2 LOWER than expected would indicate respiratory alkalosis, but 30 is higher than the expected 26, not lower. The direction is reversed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Winter's formula does not apply because the primary disorder is respiratory; the low PaCO2 is the primary problem
choices.rationale: Incorrect. The low HCO3- with acidemia identifies a primary metabolic acidosis (DKA), for which Winter's formula is precisely the validated tool to assess expected respiratory compensation.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Expected PaCO2 equals the last two digits of the pH (22), so 30 indicates severe over-compensation
choices.rationale: Incorrect. The 'last two digits of pH' shortcut is an alternate estimate of expected PaCO2 but it does not equal '22' here in a way that supports this reasoning, and a value above expected indicates UNDER-compensation (added respiratory acidosis), not over-compensation.

questions.explanation: Winter's formula quantifies the expected respiratory compensation for a primary metabolic acidosis: expected PaCO2 = (1.5 x HCO3-) + 8 ± 2. For HCO3- of 12: (1.5 x 12) + 8 = 26, range 24-28 mmHg. The measured PaCO2 of 30 exceeds this, so respiratory compensation is inadequate and a superimposed respiratory acidosis exists (e.g., from fatigue, opioids, or impending respiratory failure - clinically important in a tiring DKA patient). If the measured value fell below 24, a concurrent respiratory alkalosis would be present; within 24-28 indicates appropriate single-disorder compensation. Compensation never fully normalizes pH, so an appropriately compensating patient remains acidemic. This stepwise approach prevents missing a hidden second disorder.

Medicine and all rationales are correct.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old asthmatic woman under general anesthesia with a secured endotracheal tube develops rising peak inspiratory pressures and wheezing. The capnograph now shows an indistinct transition between the expiratory upstroke and plateau, with an upsloping ('shark-fin') waveform that lacks a flat alveolar plateau. Which single interpretation BEST explains this capnographic change?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Up-slanting of the expiratory upstroke with loss of plateau reflects expiratory airflow obstruction, as in bronchospasm
choices.rationale: Correct. Prolongation/up-slanting of phase II and loss of the phase III plateau (the shark-fin morphology) indicates heterogeneous, obstructed expiratory airflow - classic for bronchospasm/asthma, COPD, or a kinked/partially obstructed ETT. It fits this acutely wheezing asthmatic with high airway pressures.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The shark-fin pattern indicates esophageal intubation with no true alveolar gas exchange
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Esophageal intubation produces absent or rapidly diminishing CO2 waveforms (little to no sustained ETCO2), not a shark-fin with a measurable, sloping alveolar phase. This patient has a confirmed tracheal tube and ongoing CO2 output.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The pattern is a curare cleft, indicating returning spontaneous ventilation during partial paralysis
choices.rationale: Incorrect. A curare cleft is a sharp downward NOTCH within the plateau, not a generalized upsloping of the whole expiratory limb. The described morphology is the obstruction (shark-fin) pattern, not a cleft.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A prolonged plateau at an elevated baseline indicates rebreathing from an exhausted CO2 absorber
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Rebreathing raises the inspiratory BASELINE above zero (phase I elevation). The described abnormality is an upsloping expiratory limb with loss of plateau, not an elevated baseline.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The change reflects a sudden drop in ETCO2 toward zero, indicating a pulmonary embolism or circuit disconnection
choices.rationale: Incorrect. A sudden fall in ETCO2 toward zero suggests disconnection, obstruction with no ventilation, or a large embolus - a different abnormality. Here the waveform persists but is upsloping, indicating airflow obstruction rather than loss of ventilation or perfusion.

questions.explanation: A normal capnogram has four phases: phase I (inspiratory baseline at zero), phase II (rapid expiratory upstroke), phase III (alveolar plateau ending at ETCO2), and phase IV (inspiratory downstroke). Morphologic perturbations such as up-slanting of the expiratory upstroke or prolongation/loss of the plateau phase signify airway obstruction - bronchospasm, asthma, COPD, or a kinked endotracheal tube. The resulting 'shark-fin' appearance reflects uneven, delayed emptying of alveoli with differing time constants. Recognizing this pattern lets the anesthetist treat bronchospasm (deepen anesthesia, bronchodilators) rather than misattribute the change to disconnection, esophageal intubation, or a curare cleft - each of which has a distinct waveform signature.

The clinical stem (asthmatic, rising peak inspiratory pressures, wheezing under GA) is textbook bronchospasm and unambiguous. Rationale for A is accurate. Safe to publish.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man who received topical benzocaine before an awake fiberoptic intubation becomes cyanotic and lethargic. His SpO2 reads 85% and does not improve despite 100% oxygen by mask, while his arterial blood appears chocolate-brown. Methemoglobinemia is suspected. Which single statement BEST explains why his pulse oximeter is unreliable in this setting?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Methemoglobin absorbs light nearly equally at 660 and 940 nm, driving the absorbance ratio toward 1.0 and forcing the SpO2 to plateau near 85% regardless of true saturation
choices.rationale: Correct. As methemoglobin rises (around 30-35%), the A660/A940 absorbance ratio approaches 1.0, which conventional two-wavelength oximeters interpret as a fixed SpO2 of about 85%. The reading therefore plateaus and cannot reflect true oxygenation; co-oximetry is required.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Methemoglobin absorbs light only at 940 nm, so the oximeter overestimates saturation toward 100%
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Methemoglobin absorbs strongly at BOTH 660 and 940 nm, not exclusively at 940 nm, and the characteristic finding is a plateau near 85%, not a falsely high near-100% reading (that pattern is more typical of carboxyhemoglobin).

choices.label: C
choices.text: The oximeter is reading correctly; an SpO2 of 85% on 100% oxygen reflects true arterial hypoxemia from a shunt
choices.rationale: Incorrect. The chocolate-brown blood, refractoriness to oxygen, and benzocaine exposure point to methemoglobinemia, in which the SpO2 is artifactual rather than a true reflection of PaO2. Co-oximetry would reveal a normal PaO2 with elevated metHb.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Methemoglobin shifts the emitted wavelengths so the device measures venous rather than arterial blood
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Pulse oximetry isolates the pulsatile (arterial) signal regardless of dyshemoglobins; metHb alters absorbance, not the device's ability to gate on arterial pulsation. There is no shift to a venous measurement.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Methemoglobinemia causes a true rightward shift of the oxygen-hemoglobin curve, so 85% is an accurate saturation
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Methemoglobin actually shifts the residual normal hemoglobin's curve LEFT (impairing offloading), and more importantly the 85% reading is an instrument artifact of equal two-wavelength absorbance, not an accurate saturation value.

questions.explanation: Conventional pulse oximeters use two wavelengths - 660 nm (red) and 940 nm (infrared) - and compute saturation from the ratio of their absorbances, which is calibrated only for oxyhemoglobin and deoxyhemoglobin. Methemoglobin absorbs strongly at both wavelengths; as metHb approaches 30-35%, the A660/A940 ratio approaches 1.0, which the device maps to a fixed SpO2 of roughly 85%. The SpO2 therefore plateaus near 85% and becomes insensitive to actual oxygenation, giving a falsely optimistic or falsely low fixed value that ignores severity. Co-oximetry (which uses multiple wavelengths) is needed to quantify metHb, and treatment is methylene blue. This benzocaine-induced presentation - cyanosis unresponsive to oxygen with chocolate-brown blood and a 'stuck' SpO2 - is a classic board scenario.

The source confirms every load-bearing claim: methemoglobin absorbs strongly at BOTH 660 and 940 nm, driving the absorbance ratio toward 1.0 and producing an SpO2 plateau near 85% even on supplemental oxygen; benzocaine is a leading cause of acquired methemoglobinemia; and chocolate-brown/chocolate-colored blood is a recognized diagnostic clue. The clinical vignette (benzocaine before awake fiberoptic intubation, cyanosis, SpO2 85% unresponsive to 100% O2, chocolate-brown arterial blood) is internally consistent and classic.\n\nCorrect answer A is accurate and unambiguous, matching the source mechanism word-for-word. Distractors are all correctly false: B reverses the physics (MetHb absorbs at both wavelengths, and the device trends toward ~85%, not 100%); C wrongly calls the reading true hypoxemia despite failure to respond to O2 and chocolate blood; D invents a venous-blood mechanism that does not exist; E mislabels the 85% as a true saturation when it is an oximeter artifact (and the rightward-shift framing is incorrect — MetHb actually causes a leftward shift impairing O2 unloading). Medicine is correct, rationales are accurate, single best answer is defensible. Safe to publish.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with poorly controlled chronic hypertension undergoes general anesthesia for a laparoscopic colectomy. During induction his mean arterial pressure (MAP) transiently falls to 55 mmHg. Intracranial pressure is normal. Assuming intact cerebral autoregulation with a classically described lower autoregulatory limit, which of the following best describes cerebral blood flow (CBF) at this MAP, and why this patient may nonetheless be at risk?

choices.label: A
choices.text: CBF remains essentially constant because a MAP of 55 mmHg lies within the classic autoregulatory plateau (~50-150 mmHg), but chronic hypertension may shift the curve rightward, making him vulnerable to ischemia at this pressure
choices.rationale: Correct. The classic autoregulatory plateau maintains relatively constant CBF across a MAP of roughly 50-150 mmHg, so 55 mmHg is at the low end of that range. However, chronic hypertension shifts the autoregulation curve to the right, raising the lower limit, so a pressure tolerated by a normotensive patient may already produce hypoperfusion in this patient.

choices.label: B
choices.text: CBF rises sharply because pressures below 60 mmHg trigger maximal cerebral vasodilation and luxury perfusion
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Below the lower autoregulatory limit, vessels are already maximally dilated and CBF falls passively with pressure; it does not rise. 'Luxury perfusion' refers to flow exceeding metabolic demand after ischemia/injury, not a normal autoregulatory response to hypotension.

choices.label: C
choices.text: CBF is pressure-passive at all MAPs because cerebral autoregulation does not exist in adults
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Cerebral autoregulation is a well-established physiologic mechanism in adults; CBF is only pressure-passive outside the autoregulatory limits, not at all pressures.

choices.label: D
choices.text: CBF is maintained and chronic hypertension shifts the curve leftward, making him more tolerant of hypotension
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Chronic hypertension shifts the autoregulatory curve to the RIGHT (higher limits), not left. The direction is reversed, so this patient is less tolerant—not more tolerant—of low MAP.

choices.label: E
choices.text: CBF falls to zero at 55 mmHg because the lower autoregulatory limit equals the MAP at which perfusion ceases
choices.rationale: Incorrect. The lower autoregulatory limit is the pressure below which CBF begins to fall passively, not the pressure at which flow ceases. CBF does not drop to zero at 55 mmHg; critical ischemia occurs at far lower flows/pressures.

questions.explanation: Cerebral autoregulation keeps CBF relatively constant across a MAP of approximately 50-150 mmHg (CPP ~50-150 mmHg at normal ICP). Below the lower limit, cerebral vessels are maximally dilated and CBF becomes pressure-passive. Crucially, chronic hypertension shifts the entire curve to the right, raising both the lower and upper limits; a MAP of 55 mmHg that is safe for a normotensive patient may already be below the autoregulatory threshold in a chronically hypertensive patient, risking ischemia. This is why anesthetic blood-pressure targets should be individualized (often kept closer to the patient's baseline) in chronic hypertension.

It explicitly states CBF remains relatively unchanged across MAP 50-150 mmHg, so a transient MAP of 55 mmHg lies within the classic autoregulatory plateau and CBF stays essentially constant. It also confirms that in chronic hypertension the autoregulatory setpoint shifts so these patients require higher MAP goals and are vulnerable to cerebral ischemia at pressures that would be safe in normotensive patients (the textbook rightward shift) — matching option A's reasoning. Distractors are all clearly false: B (no luxury hyperperfusion below 60), C (adult autoregulation does exist), D (hypertension shifts rightward, not leftward, making patients LESS hypotension-tolerant — the opposite), E (lower limit is not where perfusion ceases; CBF does not fall to zero at 55). Medicine is accurate, the stem is precisely worded ("classically described lower limit," "intact autoregulation," "normal ICP") to make A uniquely correct, and rationales are sound.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old man is admitted to the ICU after a severe traumatic brain injury (GCS 6) sustained in a motorcycle collision. An intraparenchymal ICP monitor reads 24 mmHg and his MAP is 80 mmHg. There is no evidence of cerebral ischemia on monitoring. According to Brain Trauma Foundation-based critical care management, which of the following is the most appropriate hemodynamic and ICP target?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Treat the ICP (currently >20 mmHg) and maintain CPP in the 60-70 mmHg range, avoiding aggressive elevation of CPP above 70 mmHg with fluids and pressors because of ARDS risk
choices.rationale: Correct. ICP-lowering therapy is indicated for sustained ICP above ~20 mmHg. The recommended CPP target is 60-70 mmHg, and aggressively driving CPP above 70 mmHg with fluids/vasopressors (in the absence of ischemia) should be avoided because of the risk of ARDS.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Leave the ICP untreated and drive CPP above 90 mmHg with aggressive fluids and vasopressors to maximize cerebral oxygen delivery
choices.rationale: Incorrect. An ICP of 24 mmHg exceeds the ~20 mmHg treatment threshold and should be addressed. Aggressively pushing CPP above 70 mmHg with fluids/pressors increases ARDS risk and is specifically discouraged in the absence of ischemia.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Target a CPP of 40-50 mmHg, which is the adult goal for severe TBI
choices.rationale: Incorrect. A CPP of 40-50 mmHg is the pediatric range; the adult target is 60-70 mmHg. A CPP this low in an adult risks cerebral ischemia.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Induce systemic hypertension to a MAP of 130 mmHg and accept any resulting ICP because pressure passively protects the brain
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Inducing severe hypertension can worsen cerebral edema and ICP and provides no autoregulatory benefit. ICP must still be controlled; the brain is not 'passively protected' by extreme MAP.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Withhold all ICP treatment until ICP exceeds 40 mmHg, then begin therapy
choices.rationale: Incorrect. The treatment threshold for intracranial hypertension is approximately 20 mmHg, not 40 mmHg. Waiting until 40 mmHg permits prolonged secondary brain injury.

questions.explanation: In severe TBI, the priorities are preventing secondary injury by treating intracranial hypertension (ICP-lowering measures initiated for ICP above ~20 mmHg) and preserving cerebral perfusion. The recommended CPP target is 60-70 mmHg. Importantly, in the absence of cerebral ischemia, aggressive attempts to raise CPP above 70 mmHg using fluids and vasopressors are discouraged because they increase the risk of ARDS. A CPP of 40-50 mmHg is a pediatric target, not adult.
